title: A general thermodynamic approach for diffusion on a lattice
authors: Matías A. Di Muro, Miguel Hoyuelos
abstract: This work presents a general thermodynamic approach to describe particle diffusion on a lattice, a model used to study transport processes in solids and on surfaces. By treating each lattice site as an open thermodynamic system, the effects of microscopic particle interactions are represented through the chemical potential. A fundamental relationship between the Onsager matrix ($L$) and its ideal-system counterpart ($L_\text{id}$, where interactions are neglected) using the determinant of the covariance matrix is demonstrated. This framework allows for the calculation of transport coefficients using the combination of their ideal values and thermodynamic properties. The general result is successfully applied to reproduce the Darken equation for substitutional diffusion in solids and to derive the non-diagonal diffusion matrix of the Zhdanov model for surface diffusion of Langmuir particles. In the last case, analytical predictions are further validated through numerical simulations across various interaction potentials.
summary: This paper provides a general method to calculate diffusion coefficients in interacting systems by relating them to known ideal-system values through thermodynamic geometry. It offers a computationally efficient alternative to expensive molecular dynamics for predicting transport properties in solids and surfaces.
main_problem: Developing a general thermodynamic framework to determine the transport coefficients (Onsager matrix) for interacting particle diffusion on a lattice.
main_result: The authors derive a fundamental relationship where the Onsager matrix of an interacting system is the ideal-system matrix scaled by the ratio of the determinants of their covariance matrices. This framework successfully reproduces the Darken equation for solids and the Zhdanov model for surface diffusion.
method: The study employs classical irreversible thermodynamics, Einstein's fluctuation theory, and information geometry (using Massieu functions and Hessian matrices). Results are validated using Monte Carlo simulations.
model_or_system: A lattice-gas model where each site is treated as an open thermodynamic system. The system includes binary mixtures of species A and B with various interaction potentials, including soft-core and power-law chemical potentials.
key_observables: Onsager matrix (L), transport coefficients, diffusion currents (intrinsic, tracer, and collective), and thermodynamic factor (Gamma).
important_parameters: Coverage (theta), chemical potential (mu), temperature (T), and the degree of particle interaction (represented by the excess chemical potential).
main_assumptions: Assumes local thermal equilibrium, small thermodynamic gradients (linear response regime), and that the range of interactions is much smaller than the cell size. It also neglects higher-order terms in the entropy expansion.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of particle transfer between cells; Figure 2 displays steady-state coverage profiles on a 1D lattice; Figures 3 and 4 show diffusion currents as a function of coverage for various interaction models, demonstrating agreement between theory and simulation.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the thermodynamic framework and the relationship between transition rates and entropy; it then develops the scaling relation for the Onsager matrix using the covariance matrix determinant; finally, it applies this theory to specific models (Darken and Zhdanov) and validates it via Monte Carlo simulations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Scheme of the transitions A →B and B →A where a quantity ⃗δ is transported between cells L and R.

FIG. 2. Coverage profile of a 1D lattice of size L at the steady state, of particles of type A (black dots) and B (red diamonds). At i = 0 the type-A and B particle coverage is fixed at parameters cA and cB, respectively, while for i = L is fixed at dA and dB. The slopes are equal, given by Eq. (66). In this particular case, we have Nm = 600 and a mean coverage ¯θ = 0.4. The results were obtained from Monte Carlo simulations.

FIG. 3. Diffusion current jD for soft-core interaction as a function of the coverage θ, where j∗ D = DidθD∂xθ. The dashed line represents Eq. (70) and the symbols correspond to the Monte Carlo simulation results. The statistical error of the data is less than 1%.

FIG. 4. Diffusion current jD as a function of the coverage θ, where j∗ D = DidθD∂xθ. Linear, cuadratic and cubic chemical potentials are shown: µe = θk; k = 1 (circles), k = 2 (boxes), k = 3 (triangles). The dashed line represents Eq. (75) and the symbols correspond to the MC simulations. The statistical error of the data is less than 1%.
